entry.title: API Journal Entry-Clearing (asynchronous) the interface call failed;
entry.content:
Question:SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
Calling SOAP Journal Entry-Clearing (asynchronous) API, how to pass in parameters in ABAP Development Tools, and the interface call failed? The call attempts the following three ways, all of which report errors, as follows:
Situation 1:
Use standard SAP_COM_0002, and use service _ id: journalentryBulkclearingrequest _ in.
Error in calling asynchronous SOAP interface: The communication protocol does not exist.

1-11-2Situation 2:
Use standard SAP_COM_0002, and use service _ id: journalentryburkclearningquestions _ webi.
Error in calling asynchronous SOAP interface: The communication protocol does not exist.

Situation 3：
Use standard SAP_COM_0002 and service _ id: co _ fins _ journal _ entry _ bulk _ cle _ sprx. Error in calling asynchronous SOAP interface: Logical port does not belong to consumer agent.

entry.title: Re: Why is help.sap.com not accessible to ai agents?
entry.content:
TL;DR: It doesn't. It block pages that are decades out of date. 

Hello @christian_noack ,

Not an SAP employee, but I think I have an answer for you.

Are agents allowed to read help.sap.com?

Yes. 

The robots.txt file contains restrictions like :

User-agent: ChatGPT-User
Allow: /
Disallow: /*/frameset.htm
Disallow: /doc/*.htm$

That means that ChatGPT-User can read everything, except files that looks like this:

https://help.sap.com/docs/7d68f1a4.htm

$ = anchors the end of the URL (path + optional query, depending on the crawler). With $, the match must end right there.

The htm extension is ancient history by this point, but back in the 90s, operating systems didn't support an extension more than three letters. 
Note, SAP has been in business since the 80s, so it's not unthinkable that some .htm files still exist ✨somewhere✨, and by now they'll be extremely out of date. 
Robots.txt is used for SEO as well as bots, so you can imagine why SAP doesn't want a user-guide from 1992 showing up in search results. 

So, the site may have originally had .htm page, and after switching to .html, they wanted to make sure only new documents were indexed. 

"In these cases the information I get is often supar and I don't know of an alternative"

That being said, yes - I think a lot of people will agree that AI in does not answer SAP questions very well, even though it has access to the documentation.

AI would be god awful if it only had access to the official Python documentation, because it's very general and doesn't solve any one problem in particular. What makes AI good at Python is the absolute landslide of articles, blogs, git hub repos, etc. of Python projects that the AI has been able to index. 

The answers to your questions are subpar, simply because there is only a fraction of the material available for SAP products, compared to other software solutions.

"...what should I do instead?"

Well, I don't mean to be rude at all, but you could read the documentation?
It's a bit like asking

"a book I'm interested in is not available as an audiobook? What should I do?"

You'll just have to read the book yourself. 

Again, I'm not trying to be a dick, but I'm not really sure what else to say.

entry.title: Re: How to send dunning letters to customer Standard E-Mail address
entry.content:
Hello @Chris1973,

Well noted. Thank you for the hint.

In case I leave “Email Recipient” completely empty I get following error message: > Sample dunning printout D 0005002311 : No output possible for output item '1' due to inconsistencies. Item '1' : no email recipient has been maintained. No output possible for output item '3' due to inconsistencies. Item '3' : no email recipient has been maintained. Dunning notice for acct: D 0005002311 was created successfully (print request)

I also tried to set up like that: Output type = DUNN_NOTICE (Dunning Notice)

Email Type Code = (TO (to)

Email Address = blank

In this case I get following error message: DT_EMAIL_RECEIVER (Expression) : Mandatory input missing in row 1/column 'Email Address'(EMAIL_URI)

Could be determination step receiver also be relevant? Here I have follwoing settings:

Output type = DUNN_NOTICE (Dunning Notice)

Account Type = D (Customer) & K (Supplier)

Role = D & K

Exclusive Indicator: false

Please note that we would also like to be included in the BCC. This has been removed for the time being.

Thanks

BR Christoph

entry.title: Common Inventory Audit Challenges During Stock Taking
entry.content:
Hello Experts,

We are preparing for our periodic physical inventory (stock taking) and would like to understand the common challenges organizations face during inventory audits.

Some issues we are currently concerned about include:

  • Differences between system stock and physical stock
  • Counting errors during manual stock taking
  • Incorrect storage locations or bin issues
  • Handling damaged, obsolete, or expired materials
  • Stock in transit during the audit period
  • Operational disruptions during counting
  • Data entry or posting delays

We would appreciate insights on:

  • What are the most common inventory audit challenges in real-world scenarios?
  • Best practices to minimize discrepancies during stock taking
  • Recommended controls or procedures to ensure audit accuracy
  • Any SAP-specific tips for physical inventory processes

Thank you in advance for your guidance.

Best regards,
Stockount

entry.title: SAP Generative AI Hub: RAG on SAP Data with HANA Cloud Vector Store (Part 4 of 6)
entry.content:
SAP COMMUNITY  |  SERIES: PART 4 OF 6  |  SAP AI CORE  |  HANA CLOUD  |  RAG

SAP Generative AI Hub: RAG on SAP Data with HANA Cloud Vector Store

End-to-end RAG pipeline on SAP data — REAL_VECTOR schema setup, SAP embedding model, document chunking, similarity search, and four working use cases: knowledge base search, data dictionary retrieval, transport log search, and master data context retrieval.

By Deepa Dorairaj   |  SAP AI Solution Architect  |  Part 4 of 6  |  Published 2026

TL;DR: HANA Cloud's native REAL_VECTOR data type turns your existing SAP database into a vector store — no separate infrastructure required. Combined with the SAP embedding model through AI Core and the Generative AI Hub, you get a fully BTP-native RAG pipeline on your SAP data. This article covers the complete path: schema setup, embedding generation, chunking strategy, similarity search, and four SAP-specific RAG use cases with working code.

Series Context

This is Part 4 of a 6-part series on SAP Generative AI Hub. Parts 1–3 covered setup, Python SDK integration, and prompt pipelines. This part connects those pipelines to persistent SAP data through retrieval-augmented generation — the pattern that makes foundation model outputs grounded in your actual SAP context rather than general training data.

RAG is the architectural bridge between the prompt pipelines from Part 3 and the production SAP applications coming in Parts 5 and 6. Understanding it here makes the CAP and Joule integration articles that follow significantly easier to follow.

WHY RAG FOR SAP WORKLOADS 

Why Foundation Models Need RAG in SAP Contexts

Foundation models — even the most capable ones — have no knowledge of your SAP landscape. They do not know your custom Z-tables, your transport history, your material master conventions, your configured process variants, or the accumulated institutional knowledge in your SAP documentation. When you ask a foundation model an SAP question without context, it answers from general training data. That is not good enough for enterprise use.

RAG solves this by retrieving relevant context from your own SAP data at query time and passing it to the model alongside the question. The model's answer is grounded in your actual system — your data, your configuration, your history.

HANA Cloud makes this particularly clean for SAP practitioners because the vector store lives in the same database as your SAP data. There is no separate vector database to provision, secure, or maintain. Your embeddings sit alongside your operational data in HANA, with the same access controls, backup policies, and compliance posture you already manage.

SAP data sources → Chunk + embed → Store in HANA REAL_VECTOR → Query similarity search → Grounded model response

STEP 1: HANA VECTOR STORE SCHEMA SETUP 

Creating the REAL_VECTOR Schema in HANA Cloud

What REAL_VECTOR Is

REAL_VECTOR is a native HANA Cloud data type introduced for vector storage. It stores fixed-dimension floating-point arrays — the numerical representations (embeddings) that capture the semantic meaning of text. HANA's vector engine supports cosine similarity and L2 distance calculations directly in SQL, which means your similarity search runs inside the database without moving data out.

REAL_VECTOR is a HANA Cloud feature — it is not available in on-premise SAP HANA. Confirm your HANA Cloud instance version supports vector engine capabilities before proceeding. As of 2026, vector store functionality requires HANA Cloud QRC 1/2024 or later.

Schema Creation

Create a dedicated schema for your vector store tables. Keeping vector store tables separate from operational SAP data makes access control and maintenance significantly cleaner.

-- Create dedicated schema for vector store
CREATE SCHEMA AI_VECTOR_STORE;
 
-- General purpose document embedding table
CREATE TABLE AI_VECTOR_STORE.DOCUMENT_EMBEDDINGS (
    EMBEDDING_ID    NVARCHAR(100)   NOT NULL PRIMARY KEY,
    SOURCE_TYPE     NVARCHAR(50)    NOT NULL,  -- e.g. 'KB', 'DDIC', 'TRANSPORT'
    SOURCE_ID       NVARCHAR(200)   NOT NULL,  -- original record identifier
    CHUNK_INDEX     INTEGER         NOT NULL,  -- chunk position within source doc
    CHUNK_TEXT      NCLOB           NOT NULL,  -- original text of this chunk
    EMBEDDING       REAL_VECTOR(1536),         -- embedding vector (1536 dims)
    METADATA        NCLOB,                     -- JSON metadata for filtering
    CREATED_AT      TIMESTAMP       DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
);
 
-- Index for fast source type filtering before vector search
CREATE INDEX IDX_DOC_EMB_SOURCE ON AI_VECTOR_STORE.DOCUMENT_EMBEDDINGS (SOURCE_TYPE, SOURCE_ID);

The EMBEDDING column dimension (1536) must match the output dimension of your embedding model exactly. The SAP embedding model used in this series outputs 1536-dimensional vectors. If you change embedding models, you must recreate the table with the correct dimension — existing embeddings cannot be reused with a different model.

STEP 2: GENERATING EMBEDDINGS VIA AI CORE 

Generating Embeddings with the SAP Embedding Model

Why the SAP Embedding Model

The SAP embedding model available through AI Core's Generative AI Hub is tuned for enterprise and business content — the kind of domain-specific language that appears in SAP documentation, functional specs, transport descriptions, and master data. For SAP-specific RAG applications, it consistently outperforms general-purpose embedding models on retrieval precision for SAP terminology and process language.

Using it through the Hub also keeps the entire pipeline within the BTP trust boundary — the same governance, audit logging, and data residency controls that apply to your generative AI calls apply to embedding generation too.

Document Chunking Strategy

Chunking — splitting source documents into segments before embedding — is one of the most consequential decisions in a RAG pipeline. Chunks that are too large dilute the embedding signal. Chunks that are too small lose context. For SAP content, the following strategy produced the best retrieval results across our four use cases:

 

Source Type

Chunk Size

Chunking Approach

SAP Knowledge Base articles

500–700 tokens per chunk

Paragraph boundaries — keep procedural steps together

HANA data dictionary descriptions

Full field/table description as single chunk

Short content — no chunking needed

Transport / change log entries

One transport record per chunk

Natural boundary — each transport is self-contained

Master data descriptions

One record per chunk with key fields as prefix

Prefix with MATNR/LIFNR for retrieval context

Embedding Generation Code

# Step 2: Generate embeddings using SAP embedding model via AI Core
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.native.openai import OpenAI
import hdbcli.dbapi as hdb
import json, uuid
 
# Initialize SDK client — credentials from environment or config file
client = OpenAI()
 
# Connect to HANA Cloud
conn = hdb.connect(
    address='<your-hana-host>.hanacloud.ondemand.com',
    port=443, user='<user>', password='<password>', encrypt=True
)
 
def generate_embedding(text: str) -> list:
    """Generate embedding vector using SAP embedding model via AI Core."""
    response = client.embeddings.create(
        model='text-embedding-ada-002',
        # SAP-provided embedding model — routed through AI Core
        input=text
    )
    return response.data[0].embedding
 
def chunk_text(text: str, chunk_size: int = 600, overlap: int = 50) -> list:
    """Split text into overlapping chunks by word count."""
    words = text.split()
    chunks = []
    for i in range(0, len(words), chunk_size - overlap):
        chunk = ' '.join(words[i:i + chunk_size])
        if chunk:
            chunks.append(chunk)
    return chunks
 
def store_embeddings(source_type: str, source_id: str,
                     text: str, metadata: dict = None):
    """Chunk, embed, and store a document in HANA vector store."""
    chunks = chunk_text(text)
    cursor = conn.cursor()
 
    for idx, chunk in enumerate(chunks):
        embedding = generate_embedding(chunk)
        embedding_id = str(uuid.uuid4())
 
        cursor.execute('''
            INSERT INTO AI_VECTOR_STORE.DOCUMENT_EMBEDDINGS
            (EMBEDDING_ID, SOURCE_TYPE, SOURCE_ID, CHUNK_INDEX,
             CHUNK_TEXT, EMBEDDING, METADATA)
            VALUES (?, ?, ?, ?, ?, TO_REAL_VECTOR(?), ?)
        ''', (
            embedding_id, source_type, source_id, idx,
            chunk, str(embedding),
            json.dumps(metadata) if metadata else None
        ))
 
    conn.commit()
    print(f'Stored {len(chunks)} chunks for {source_type}/{source_id}')

Embedding generation has a cost per token through AI Core. For initial ingestion of large SAP document sets, run embedding generation in batches with rate limiting and store results immediately — do not regenerate embeddings on every pipeline run. Re-embed only when source documents change.

STEP 3: SIMILARITY SEARCH IN HANA SQL 

Querying the Vector Store with COSINE_SIMILARITY

How HANA Vector Search Works

HANA Cloud's vector engine computes similarity between a query embedding and all stored embeddings directly in SQL using built-in functions. COSINE_SIMILARITY measures the angle between vectors — returning values between -1 and 1, where 1 is identical. For SAP document retrieval, cosine similarity consistently outperforms L2 distance because it is scale-invariant — document length differences do not artificially inflate distance scores.

Similarity Search Function

# Step 3: Similarity search — retrieve top-k most relevant chunks
def similarity_search(query: str, source_type: str = None,
                      top_k: int = 5) -> list:
    """
    Embed query and retrieve most similar chunks from HANA vector store.
    Optionally filter by source_type for targeted retrieval.
    """
    query_embedding = generate_embedding(query)
    cursor = conn.cursor()
 
    # Build query with optional source type filter
    source_filter = "AND SOURCE_TYPE = ?" if source_type else ""
    params = [str(query_embedding), top_k]
    if source_type:
        params.insert(1, source_type)
 
    cursor.execute(f'''
        SELECT TOP ?
            SOURCE_TYPE,
            SOURCE_ID,
            CHUNK_INDEX,
            CHUNK_TEXT,
            METADATA,
            COSINE_SIMILARITY(EMBEDDING, TO_REAL_VECTOR(?)) AS SIMILARITY_SCORE
        FROM AI_VECTOR_STORE.DOCUMENT_EMBEDDINGS
        WHERE 1=1 {source_filter}
        ORDER BY SIMILARITY_SCORE DESC
    ''', params)
 
    results = []
    for row in cursor.fetchall():
        results.append({
            'source_type': row[0],
            'source_id':   row[1],
            'chunk_index': row[2],
            'chunk_text':  row[3],
            'metadata':    json.loads(row[4]) if row[4] else {},
            'score':       float(row[5])
        })
    return results

Filter by SOURCE_TYPE before running vector similarity whenever you know which data domain is relevant to the query. Narrowing the search space improves both retrieval precision and query performance — especially as your vector store grows across multiple SAP data domains.

STEP 4: THE COMPLETE RAG PIPELINE 

Assembling the End-to-End RAG Pipeline

With embedding generation and similarity search in place, the RAG pipeline itself is straightforward: embed the query, retrieve relevant chunks, assemble them as context, and pass context plus query to the foundation model. The model's response is grounded in your retrieved SAP data.

# Step 4: Complete RAG pipeline function
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.langchain.openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain.schema import HumanMessage, SystemMessage
 
llm = ChatOpenAI(proxy_model_name='claude-3.5-sonnet')
 
def rag_query(question: str, source_type: str = None,
              top_k: int = 5, system_context: str = None) -> str:
    """
    Full RAG pipeline: retrieve relevant SAP context,
    then generate grounded response using foundation model.
    """
    # Step 1: Retrieve relevant context from HANA vector store
    retrieved = similarity_search(question, source_type, top_k)
 
    # Step 2: Assemble context from retrieved chunks
    context_blocks = []
    for r in retrieved:
        context_blocks.append(
            f"[Source: {r['source_type']} / {r['source_id']} ",
            f"| Score: {r['score']:.3f}]\n{r['chunk_text']}"
        )
    context = '\n\n---\n\n'.join(context_blocks)
 
    # Step 3: Build grounded prompt with retrieved context
    default_system = '''You are an SAP expert assistant.
    Answer questions using ONLY the context provided below.
    If the context does not contain enough information to answer,
    say so explicitly — do not use general knowledge.
    Always reference the source when citing specific information.'''
 
    messages = [
        SystemMessage(content=system_context or default_system),
        HumanMessage(content=f'''
CONTEXT FROM SAP DATA:\n{context}
\n\nQUESTION: {question}''')
    ]
 
    # Step 4: Generate grounded response
    return llm(messages).content

The instruction 'Answer using ONLY the context provided — do not use general knowledge' is critical for SAP RAG pipelines. Without it, the model supplements retrieved context with training data, which can introduce information about standard SAP that contradicts your specific configuration or customizations.

FOUR SAP RAG USE CASES 

Use Case Examples

  ▶  Use Case 1: SAP Knowledge Base Search 

Grounded Q&A on SAP Documentation

Ingest your SAP knowledge base articles, configuration guides, and internal runbooks into the vector store. The RAG pipeline then answers practitioner questions grounded in your actual documented procedures — not generic SAP guidance from the model's training data.

# Ingest SAP knowledge base articles
kb_articles = [
    {
        'id': 'KB-2024-001',
        'title': 'HANA Delta Load: Handling Large Volume Tables',
        'content': '''When delta loading tables exceeding 500M records...
        Use hash-based partitioning with MOD(ABS(TO_BIGINT(KEY_FIELD)), N)
        to distribute load across parallel workers. Monitor memory via
        M_HEAP_MEMORY view. Control table must track per-partition watermarks.'''
    },
]
 
for article in kb_articles:
    store_embeddings(
        source_type='KB',
        source_id=article['id'],
        text=f"{article['title']}\n\n{article['content']}",
        metadata={'title': article['title']}
    )
 
# Query the knowledge base
answer = rag_query(
    question='How should I handle integer overflow in HANA delta loads?',
    source_type='KB',
    top_k=3
)
print(answer)

  ▶  Use Case 2: HANA Data Dictionary Search 

Natural Language Queries on Table and Field Metadata

Embed HANA data dictionary metadata — table descriptions, field definitions, domain values, foreign key relationships — so practitioners can find relevant tables and fields using natural language instead of needing to know exact technical names upfront.

# Ingest HANA data dictionary metadata from HANA system views
cursor = conn.cursor()
cursor.execute('''
    SELECT T.TABLE_NAME, T.COMMENTS,
           STRING_AGG(C.COLUMN_NAME || ': ' || IFNULL(C.COMMENTS,''),  ', ')
           AS FIELD_LIST
    FROM SYS.TABLES T
    JOIN SYS.TABLE_COLUMNS C ON T.TABLE_NAME = C.TABLE_NAME
    WHERE T.SCHEMA_NAME = 'SAPHANADB'
    AND T.TABLE_NAME LIKE 'Z%'
    -- Scope to your custom Z-tables first
    GROUP BY T.TABLE_NAME, T.COMMENTS
    LIMIT 500
''')
 
for row in cursor.fetchall():
    table_name, description, fields = row
    content = f'Table: {table_name}\nDescription: {description}\nFields: {fields}'
    store_embeddings(
        source_type='DDIC',
        source_id=table_name,
        text=content,
        metadata={'table_name': table_name}
    )
 
# Find relevant tables using natural language
answer = rag_query(
    question='Which tables store customer payment terms and credit limits?',
    source_type='DDIC',
    top_k=5
)
print(answer)

  ▶  Use Case 3: Transport and Change Log Retrieval 

Semantic Search Across SAP Change History

Transport request descriptions and change logs are high-value institutional knowledge that is almost always searched by keyword — missing semantic connections between related changes. Embedding transport history enables semantic retrieval: find transports related to a concept or problem even when the exact terms don't match.

# Ingest transport descriptions from E070 / E07T
cursor.execute('''
    SELECT T.TRKORR, T.AS4TEXT, T.AS4DATE, T.AS4USER,
           STRING_AGG(O.OBJECT || '/' || O.OBJ_NAME, ', ') AS OBJECTS
    FROM E070 T
    JOIN E071 O ON T.TRKORR = O.TRKORR
    WHERE T.AS4DATE >= '20250101'
    AND T.TRSTATUS = 'R'
    -- Released transports only
    GROUP BY T.TRKORR, T.AS4TEXT, T.AS4DATE, T.AS4USER
''')
 
for row in cursor.fetchall():
    tr_id, description, date, user, objects = row
    content = f'''Transport: {tr_id}
Description: {description}
Changed by: {user} on {date}
Objects: {objects}'''
    store_embeddings(
        source_type='TRANSPORT',
        source_id=tr_id,
        text=content,
        metadata={'tr_id': tr_id, 'date': str(date), 'user': user}
    )
 
# Semantic search across transport history
answer = rag_query(
    question='What changes were made to the FI posting handler in 2025?',
    source_type='TRANSPORT',
    top_k=5
)
print(answer)

  ▶  Use Case 4: Master Data Context Retrieval for AI Classification 

Retrieval-Augmented Master Data Classification

This use case combines the master data classification pipeline from Part 3 with RAG — retrieving similar previously-classified records as few-shot examples at query time. The model classifies new records guided by real examples from your own SAP master data, not generic training examples. This produces significantly higher accuracy on company-specific classification schemes.

# Ingest already-classified master data records as reference examples
cursor.execute('''
    SELECT MATNR, MAKTX, MATKL, MTART, ZZCATEGORY
    FROM MARA JOIN MAKT ON MARA.MATNR = MAKT.MATNR
    WHERE MAKT.SPRAS = 'E'
    AND ZZCATEGORY IS NOT NULL
    -- Only records with confirmed classifications
    LIMIT 1000
''')
 
for row in cursor.fetchall():
    matnr, desc, matkl, mtart, category = row
    content = f'Material: {matnr} | {desc} | Type: {mtart} | Category: {category}'
    store_embeddings(
        source_type='MASTER_DATA',
        source_id=matnr,
        text=content,
        metadata={'matnr': matnr, 'category': category, 'mtart': mtart}
    )
 
# Classify new material using retrieved similar records as examples
new_material = 'Hydraulic seal kit 250bar SAP-HYD-250'
 
answer = rag_query(
    question=f'Classify this new material: {new_material}',
    source_type='MASTER_DATA',
    top_k=5,
    system_context='''You are an SAP master data classifier.
    Use the similar records provided as classification examples.
    Return JSON only: {"category": "...", "confidence": "High/Medium/Low",
    "based_on": [list of similar MATNR values used as reference]}'''
)
print(answer)

Retrieval-augmented classification using your own historical master data consistently outperforms zero-shot classification because the model is guided by your specific category labels and conventions — not generic industry taxonomies. The more classified records you ingest, the more accurate the retrieval and the better the classification output.

DESIGN CONSIDERATIONS 

What to Get Right Before Going to Production

 

Consideration

Guidance

Embedding consistency

Never mix embedding models across records in the same table. A similarity search comparing vectors from different models produces meaningless scores. Pick one model and stick to it — if you change models, re-embed everything.

Chunk overlap

50-token overlap between chunks prevents relevant context from being split across chunk boundaries. Adjust overlap up for dense technical content like ABAP code or configuration tables.

Score thresholds

Not all retrieved chunks are relevant. Filter results below a COSINE_SIMILARITY score of 0.75 before passing to the model. Low-score chunks add noise and reduce answer quality.

Re-embedding cadence

SAP data changes. Transport descriptions, knowledge base articles, and master data classifications go stale. Build a delta re-embedding job that runs on the same schedule as your delta data loads.

Source type filtering

Always filter by SOURCE_TYPE when the query domain is known. Mixing transport history chunks with knowledge base chunks in a single retrieval pass produces lower precision than targeted retrieval from the right domain.

What's Next in This Series

  • Part 5: Integrating AI Core into a BTP CAP Application — production-grade AI with OData APIs, S/4HANA integration, and the RAG pipeline from this article embedded in a CAP service
  • Part 6: Extending SAP Joule with Foundation Models — custom Joule skills backed by AI Core, with HANA vector store retrieval for grounded natural language interaction across SAP applications

The vector store you build in this article carries forward into Parts 5 and 6. If you're following the series end-to-end, the RAG pipeline here is the foundation that makes the CAP application and Joule extension in the next two parts genuinely useful rather than demo-grade. Drop a comment if you hit issues with REAL_VECTOR column dimensions or similarity score thresholds — these are the two most common friction points in production deployments.

entry.title: Re: Onboarding 1.0 Decommissioning Procedure & FAQ

entry.title: Re: Resource Handover for Product WTs in S/4HANA Cloud Pu...
entry.content:
Dear Chris,

thank you for your detailed response.

"The standard approach is to design the movement with LOSC, an intermediate step, or two separate tasks."

So if I get it correctly, I have to introduce HUs in case of every movement where I would like to make this resource handover activity work.
The problem with the two seperate task is that I lose the ability to use min-max-based replenishment functionality.

My best alternative idea to solve this issue, even before I posted my question, was to create a "transfer" storage type, from where I trigger stock replenishment, and from there, I will do a putaway as a separate 2nd step with a separate WO. But on this intermediate "transfer" Storage type I cannot consider the existing stock amount at my destination storage type.

If I assume correctly, to keep full replenishment functionality and use still use separate WTs (for different resources), my only chance is HU-based LOSC?

Thank you again!

Best regards, 
Adam

entry.title: Re: Week 3 : Use RAP Business Logic Prediction to Implement Validation
entry.content: Week 3 Completed

entry.title: Re: Scrapping / Issuance of Expired Batches
entry.content:
Keep the message as it is an Error. Go to tcode OMJ5 and make the entry for movement type 551 = 3 (no check at goods issue).

This will allow you to scrap the expired stock while still preventing other movements on the stock.

entry.title: Re: Onboarding 1.0 Decommissioning Procedure & FAQ
entry.content:
@DaveA thanks for your excellent questions -- please see my clarified blog above, with the extended Preview availability, clarified in my blog. Preview will still be available until the 1H 2026 Production Release date, the same updated date shared in our webinar session recently.

@kenjodrey6 Onboarding 1.0 will still be available as an option in the Main Menu, but will have a "locked" state screen when trying to access it. It will be removed from the main navigation at a later date, broadly, for all customers who may still have it in their Main Menu.

@wens you should be able to proceed with decommission presently, and once decommissioned properly, Onboarding 1.0 should no longer be present as an option within HCM broadly. Configuring and setting up Onboarding 1.0 enables this functionality HCM-wide.

entry.title: Re: Manufacturer Part Number; not visible in Purchase Req...
entry.content:
Dear @Adbo,

In Public Cloud the recommended solution is to make Manufacturer Part Number (MPN) available in the purchase requisition through in-app extensibility. SAP documents MPN as part of the standard product master data model under ProductManufacturerNumber and SAP also documents Manufacturer part number as a supported attribute in the purchase requisition item integration model. This confirms that MPN is available as a standard manufacturer-related attribute in the Public Cloud data model and can be used in the purchase requisition integration model

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/bb9f1469daf04bd894ab2167f8132a1a/f20f62544f6b4188b55b913b47ccd118.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/3c916ef10fc240c9afc594b346ffaf77/2c973258fb9d2060e10000000a44147b.html

For the purchase requisition apps, SAP supported Public Cloud approach is to use Custom Fields with business context Procurement Purchase Requisition Item. SAP purchasing extensibility guide shows that the custom field can be enabled on the Purchase Requisition Item exposed in API/CDS and transferred further from Purchase Requisition to Purchase Order

 

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0e602d466b99490187fcbb30d1dc897c/9e9ea1e208a14d2d8e6274ffc4c3fd84.html

So:

1. Create a custom field such as Manufacturer Part Number in Custom Fields app for business context Procurement Purchase Requisition Item

2. Enable it for the PR UI and, if needed for reporting/API usage

3. Default or validate the field automatically based on the selected product/material using the released PR extensibility options. SAP documents the PR item enhancement option MM_PUR_S4_PR_MODIFY_ITEM for checking or changing values at requisition item level

This gives requisitioners direct visibility of the correct Manufacturer Part Number during PR creation, improves material precision and keeps the logic clearly separated from Supplier Part Number. It is also aligned with the clean-core approach for Public Cloud

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0e602d466b99490187fcbb30d1dc897c/720dcec59d264114bc4ec19759aa4514.html

https://fioriappslibrary.hana.ondemand.com/sap/fix/externalViewer/#/detail/Apps('F1481')/S36

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Issue with Balance Carryforward and Legacy Data Transfer Settings (CoCode Status & Fiscal Years)
entry.content:
Hi everyone,

We are currently performing the fiscal year-end closing for 2025 in an S/4HANA Public Cloud environment. Our Go-Live/Legacy Data Transfer (LDT) date was 31.05.2025.

I am facing an issue where the P&L accounts in the 2026 Trial Balance are showing cumulative balances (not cleared). To resolve this, I am trying to finalize the settings in the "Make Company Code Settings - Asset Accounting Specific" app, but I want to ensure I enter the correct values to avoid any further inconsistencies.

Could you please clarify the following for our scenario?

  1. Legacy Data Transfer Segment: Should I change the "Transfer Status" from "Ongoing" to "Completed" at this stage to allow a clean balance carryforward?
  2. Highest Fiscal Year: Since we are now in 2026, should this field be set to 2025 or 2026 to close the previous year and allow 2026 postings?
  3. Closed Fiscal Year: Under the Ledger settings, should the "Closed Fiscal Year" be set to 2024 (the year prior to our LDT)?

Our main goal is to successfully run the Balance Carryforward (FAGLGV) so that the 2026 opening balances are correct and P&L accounts are zeroed out.

Any guidance on the mandatory sequence for these settings would be greatly appreciated.

Thanks in advance!

entry.title: Unable to Delete Parked Document for Supplier Down Payment – Posting Period Error in Workflow
entry.content:
Hello Experts,

We are facing an issue related to the Supplier Down Payment workflow and would appreciate your guidance.

Scenario:

  • A user posted a Supplier Down Payment Request (Document Type: KA) through the “Verify Supplier Down Payment” app in February.

  • Both approvals were completed in March.

  • Since the original posting date is in February and the posting period for February is now closed, the workflow fails during the background posting step with a posting period error.

Error:
Posting period 002 2026 not open.

Troubleshooting Done:

  • We recreated the same scenario in the QAS system.

  • Even after opening the February posting period, the issue still occurs QAS

Requirement:
The user would like to delete the parked document that is currently stuck in the workflow.

Question:
Is there any standard way to delete or cancel this parked Supplier Down Payment document when it is stuck due to posting period issues in workflow?

Any suggestions or recommended approach would be greatly appreciated.

Thank you.

Rohini

entry.title: Re: FULL OUTER JOIN in ABAP SQL?
entry.content:
Hi,

I just tried the following in our dev system with very limited data (4-digit line count in each table). The performance was .... I just did one run.

    SELECT coalesce( ekbe~ebeln, ekkn~ebeln ) as ebeln,
           coalesce( ekbe~ebelp, ekkn~ebelp ) as ebelp,
           coalesce( ekbe~zekkn, ekkn~zekkn ) as zekkn,
           ekbe~bewtp,
           ekkn~loekz
    FROM ekbe
      LEFT OUTER JOIN ekkn
        ON    ekbe~ebeln = ekkn~ebeln
          AND ekbe~ebelp = ekkn~ebelp
          AND ekbe~zekkn = ekkn~zekkn
*    WHERE ...
    UNION
    SELECT coalesce( ekbe~ebeln, ekkn~ebeln ) as ebeln,
           coalesce( ekbe~ebelp, ekkn~ebelp ) as ebelp,
           coalesce( ekbe~zekkn, ekkn~zekkn ) as zekkn,
           ekbe~bewtp,
           ekkn~loekz
    FROM ekkn
      LEFT OUTER JOIN ekbe
        ON    ekbe~ebeln = ekkn~ebeln
          AND ekbe~ebelp = ekkn~ebelp
          AND ekbe~zekkn = ekkn~zekkn
*    WHERE ...
    INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_result).

    out->write( lt_result ).

The idea is to do both "parts" of a FULL OUTER JOIN seperately and then unite them with a UNION. This one especially without the ALL addition in order to remove the duplicates which are the datasets which exists in both tables. 

Best regards
Michael

Is there a way for us to retrieve the scores from each of these goal plans and use them separately in the calculation?

entry.title: Re: Ledger Setup and its Role in Management Accounting in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
entry.content:
ICMR and Group Reporting covers the legal requirements while Group Valuation covers the Management perspective. While it is true that both approaches require the "elimination of intercompany profits", there are differences between these two consolidations.

One example: Your group is based in Europe and you own a subsidiary in India. However, due to local laws 51% is locally owned and 49% by you. Management / steering for the subsidiary is done entirely by the group, for that reason you will want to show the subsidiary 100% in management reporting. For legal reporting you must show 49%.

entry.title: SAP FICO wthholding annual accumulation limit configuration
entry.content: In my case, I am using the withholding tax section 194C in SAP FICO. According to section 194C, the tax rules are as follows: For instance, if it is a contract payment, a single bill above 30,000 directly incurs a 1% TDS. Similarly, annually, if the total exceeds 1 lakh, that is the limit. For example, if I have four bills each of 29,000, they do not cross 1 lakh initially, and also, each bill is below 30,000, so no TDS is applied. Now, if I make the fourth bill also 29,000, then the total annual amount will exceed 1 lakh. So, I need to deduct TDS there. My question is, how do I configure this accumulation—where do I set up the tracking of these cumulative billed amounts—so that when I raise the fourth bill, it flags that the annual limit is exceeded, and TDS must be applied?

entry.title: Mapping SAP Process Orchestration JMS (IBM MQ) Adapter to SAP Integration Suite IBM MQ Adapter
entry.content:
This blog post is part of the series Master Blog: SAP Process Orchestration to SAP Integration Suite Migration – Tool and Guidance exploring how to migrate various integration patterns from SAP Process Orchestration to SAP Integration Suite using the Migration Tool.

Objective of this Blog

In this blog, we will focus on the mapping of SAP Process Orchestration JMS (IBM MQ) adapter — with Transport Protocol “WebSphereMQ (MQSeries) JMS Provider” and Message Protocol both “JMS 1.x” or “JMS 2.0”) — to SAP Integration Suite IBM MQ adapter.

Since the Migration Tool skips the SAP Process Orchestration JMS (IBM MQ) adapter, the approach described in this blog will help to manually configure both Sender and Receiver IBM MQ adapters in SAP Integration Suite iFlows based on source JMS (IBM MQ) SAP Process Orchestration scenarios.

Related Blogs 

  • IBM MQ JMS Adapter for SAP Integration Suite
  • Integrating IBM MQ On-Premise with SAP Cloud Integration Using the IBM MQ Adapter

SAP Process Orchestration JMS Adapter Modules

In SAP Process Orchestration, the following JMS Adapter Modules are commonly used to convert message payloads between binary and XML formats:                       

  • SAP XI JMS Adapter/ConvertJMSMessageToBinary
  • SAP XI JMS Adapter/ConvertBinaryToXMBMessage
  • SAP XI JMS Adapter/ConvertMessageToBinary
  • SAP XI JMS Adapter/SendBinarytoXIJMSService

In SAP Process Orchestration, these modules are often required when integrating with JMS providers such as IBM MQ. However, in SAP Integration Suite they are not required, as their functionality is provided directly by the IBM MQ adapter itself. The IBM MQ adapter in SAP Integration Suite offers built-in options for message conversion, allowing users to select either byte or text message formats directly in the adapter configuration.

Variants

Based on the SAP Process Orchestration source parameters, the migration will result in three different target variants:

- Sender

  • JMS Poll
  • JMS Subscribe

- Receiver:

  • JMS

Sender

The source Sender JMS MQ channel must have the following properties:

  • Adapter Type: JMS
  • Sender
  • Transport Protocol: WebSphereMQ (MQSeries) JMS Provider
  • Message protocol: JMS 1.x or JMS 2.0

The relevant Sender variants — JMS Poll and JMS Subscribe — are detailed below (REST variant doesn’t apply).

JMS Poll

JMS Poll implements a message retrieval mechanism where the IBM MQ JMS Sender Adapter actively polls messages from IBM MQ queues or topics at defined intervals, using a request-based approach to check for new messages periodically.

Condition

In SAP Process Orchestration, for this variant to apply, the following condition must be met:

“Use Message Listener Based Connector” checkbox in the Processing view not checked:

Mapping (SAP Process Orchestration → SAP Integration Suite)

The following table shows the mapping from SAP Process Orchestration to SAP Integration Suite:

 

SAP Process Orchestration

SAP Process Orchestration

SAP Integration Suite

SAP Integration Suite

View

6

7

10

Processing

SAP Process Orchestration

The following SAP Process Orchestration screenshots illustrate the mapping:

Source view

If “Use SSL” checked and “WSMQ Cipher Spec” = “WebSphereMQ Cipher Spec”:

If “Use SSL” checked and “WSMQ Cipher Spec” = “Other”:

Processing view

SAP Integration Suite

The following SAP Integration Suite screenshots illustrate the mapping:

Connection view

* After maintaining the Credentials in the SAP Integration Suite Security Materials section, the alias name must be provided in the Credential Name field to establish the connection.

Processing view

If “Message Destination” = “Topic”:

If “Message Destination” = “Queue”:

 JMS Subscribe

JMS Subscribe implements a message retrieval mechanism where the IBM MQ JMS Sender Adapter establishes an active subscription to IBM MQ queues or topics, enabling real-time or near real-time message delivery as soon as messages are published to the destination.

Condition

In SAP Process Orchestration, for this variant to apply, the following condition must be met:

“Use Message Listener Based Connector” checkbox in the Processing view checked:

Mapping (SAP Process Orchestration → SAP Integration Suite)

Same mapping as for the JMS Poll variant, but without fields 14, 15, and 16.

Receiver

The source Receiver JMS MQ channel must have the following properties: 

  • Adapter Type: JMS
  • Receiver
  • Transport Protocol: WebSphereMQ (MQSeries) JMS Provider
  • Message protocol: JMS 1.x or JMS 2.0

The relevant Receiver variant — JMS — is detailed below (HTTPS variant doesn’t apply):

JMS

JMS Receiver implements a message publishing mechanism where the IBM MQ JMS Receiver Adapter enables SAP Integration Suite to send messages to IBM MQ queues or topics, acting as a message producer that publishes data to designated destinations in the IBM MQ server.

Mapping (SAP Process Orchestration → SAP Integration Suite)

The following table shows the mapping from SAP Process Orchestration to SAP Integration Suite:

 

SAP Process Orchestration

SAP Process Orchestration

Delivery Mode of Message Producer

Advanced

Delivery Mode

13

Processing

JMS ReplyTo Destination Name

Advanced

ReplyTo Destination

14

Processing

JMS Message Expiry Period (msecs)

Advanced

Expiry

15

Processing

JMS Message Priority

Advanced

Priority

SAP Process Orchestration

The following SAP Process Orchestration screenshots illustrate the mapping:

Target view

If “Use SSL” checked and “WSMQ Cipher Spec” = “WebSphereMQ Cipher Spec”:

If “Use SSL” checked and “WSMQ Cipher Spec” = “Other”:

Processing view

SAP Integration Suite

The following SAP Integration Suite screenshots illustrate the mapping:

Connection view

*After maintaining the Credentials in the SAP Integration Suite Security Materials section, the alias name must be provided in the Credential Name field to establish the connection.

Processing view

If “Message Destination” = “Topic”:

If “Message Destination” = “Queue”:

Advanced view

Conclusion

By following the mapping approach outlined in this blog — including detailed mapping tables and screenshots for the different target SAP Integration Suite IBM MQ sender and receiver adapter variants — users can successfully migrate their adapters from SAP Process Orchestration scenarios to SAP Integration Suite iFlows.

 * Since not all parameters can be mapped, end users must evaluate whether any functionality configured in the source SAP Process Orchestration JMS (IBM MQ) adapter has not been migrated to the target SAP Integration Suite IBM MQ adapter (e.g., PI Settings, Error-Handling Settings, or Duplicate Handling).

** In addition, some functionalities — such as correlation ID mapping — cannot be mapped directly but can be configured in the SAP Integration Suite IBM MQ adapter. E.g., in the receiver adapter, it can be set dynamically using an exchange header or property.

entry.title: CDN Commerce API with the Composable Storefront OCC_BACKEND_BASE_URL_VALUE?
entry.content:
When putting a CDN in front of the API for SAP Commerce, the SAP Commerce Portal will have the "origin" of the API, but not the CDN's URL for the API. For example, the Portal may have "origin-api.example.com" configured to be hosted by the SAP servers, while the CDN points the public URL "api.example.com" at the origin-api.example.com.

When setting up the index.html files for the Composable Storefront, it is common practice to use <meta name="occ-backend-base-url" content="OCC_BACKEND_BASE_URL_VALUE"> which will replace the OCC_BACKEND_BASE_URL_VALUE with the first API endpoint from the portal for the environment that gets deployed to. This allows a single build from a single branch to be deployed to each environment (d1, s1, p1, etc).

However, that means that it will pick the origin-api.example.com non-CDN version of the API. So, how can the CDN URL be used? Does a special branch+build+deploy with a hard-coded occ-backend-base-url that explicity puts the CDN's api.example.com have to be used? Is that the only way?

entry.title: Re: Edit Existing Process Order Number Range Intervals or...
entry.content:
Dear @HO_IT,

In Public Cloud this requirement should be handled through configuration activity SSCUI 106461 Configure Number Range for Order Types. SAP Help states that this activity is used to define number ranges by determining internal number intervals for individual order types. In addition, SAP Note 3720445 confirms that when an order type is assigned a number range that has not been maintained the missing interval must be created in SSCUI 106461 Configure Number Range for Order Types in CBC. For process orders, SAP states that process order types are maintained in SSCUI 102801 Define Process Order Types and that every order type must be assigned a number range. SAP also states that when copying an existing process order type, you can either keep the existing number range assignment or choose a different one. Therefore the supported Public Cloud solution is to maintain the required interval in SSCUI 106461 and assign it to the relevant Process Order Types in SSCUI 102801. If the business requires different numbering logic for different scenarios the standard approach is to use separate process order types and assign different number ranges to each of them

More details below:

https://learning.sap.com/courses/configuring-sap-s-4hana-cloud-public-edition-manufacturing-execution/configuring-process-order-types

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3720445

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: SAP Enable Now goes WalkMe – Ein umfassender Rückblick auf die Roundtable Reihe 2026
entry.content:
Die dreiteilige Roundtable‑Reihe „SAP Enable Now goes WalkMe“ in Zürich, Berlin, Eschborn und im virtuellen Format markierte einen wichtigen Meilenstein für SAP Enable Now Kunden. Die Sessions boten einen sehr klaren und zugleich praxisnahen Blick auf die Transition von SAP Enable Now (SEN) nach WalkMe, die Positionierung von WalkMe Learning Arc und die Rolle von KI im modernen Enablement.

Die Diskussionen machten deutlich, dass die kommenden Jahre durch tiefgreifende Veränderungen in Lernprozessen, Content‑Erstellung und Performance Support geprägt sein werden. Viele Unternehmen stehen vor der Aufgabe, ihre bisherige Enable‑Now‑Landschaft strukturiert weiterzuführen, während sie gleichzeitig KI‑gestützte Ansätze, moderne Ausspielmechanismen und flexible Lernpfade integrieren.

Die Transition von SAP Enable Now zu WalkMe – Klarheit im Vorgehen, Chancen im Prozess

Fokus der Roundtables lag auf der Transition von SAP Enable Now zu WalkMe. Viele Teilnehmer:innen formulierten ein gemeinsames Anliegen: Planungssicherheit, Transparenz und ein Verständnis darüber, wie die Migration technisch, fachlich und organisatorisch gelingt.

SEN bleibt bis Ende 2030 in der Wartung. Für diese Zeitspanne können Inhalte weiterhin gepflegt und produziert werden. Danach stehen bestehende Lernobjekte weiterhin zur Verfügung, lassen sich jedoch nicht mehr editieren. Dadurch entsteht ein stabiler Rahmen, in dem Unternehmen die Transition bewusst gestalten und den Aufwand sinnvoll auf die einzelnen Projektphasen verteilen können. Besonders hilfreich: Wer als SEN-Bestandskunde WalkMe Learning Arc lizenziert, behält den Zugang zum SEN Producer, ohne zusätzliche Lizenzkosten!

Der technische Migrationspfad basiert auf einem API‑basierten Workspace‑Transfer. Dabei wird der bestehende SEN‑Workspace in eine Learning‑Arc‑Instanz kopiert. Die Migration erfolgt unter drei Gesichtspunkten: „hostable“, „playable“ und „editable. „Hostable“ beschreibt die Übernahme der SEN‑Inhalte in die WalkMe‑Infrastruktur. „Playable“ bedeutet, dass die alten SEN‑Inhalte auch nach der Migration lauffähig bleiben und in WalkMe Content-Strukturen eingebaut werden können. „Editable“ schließlich bezieht sich auf die Bearbeitung in WalkMe Learning Arc mit Hilfe des zur Verfügung stehenden SEN Producers.

 

Während der Übertragungsprozess zuverlässig funktioniert, überträgt er jedoch keine Versionshistorien, sondern lediglich den jeweils aktuellen Stand jedes Lernobjekts. Auch die Rollen‑ oder Segmentierungslogik muss nach dem Transfer neu erstellt werden, was auf der gleichen technologischen Grundlage erfolgt und daher keinen nennenswerten Aufwand erzeugt. Jedes Unternehmen sollte vorab definieren, welche Inhalte in welcher Struktur überführt werden sollen. Die Diskussionen der diesjährigen Roundtables zeigen, dass es sinnvoll ist, bestehende SEN‑Strukturen kritisch zu prüfen und strukturell zu entschlacken, bevor sie nach WalkMe überführt werden.

 

Die größte fachliche Abweichung betrifft den SEN Companion‑Content. In‑App‑Hilfen und Guided Tours werden nicht übertragen und müssen in WalkMe neu erstellt werden. Unternehmen, die bisher stark mit Companion gearbeitet haben, müssen daher gezielt entscheiden, wie sie den zukünftigen Performance Support gestalten. Klar ist, dass WalkMe als Marktführer im DAP Segment hier weit mehr Möglichkeiten bietet als bisher.

Um die Transition zeitlich zu entzerren, wurde die „Test‑&‑Go‑Live‑SKU“ vorgestellt. Sie ermöglicht für vier bis elf Monate einen Parallelbetrieb der beiden Systeme. Das verschafft Raum für einen kontrollierten Übergang, ohne Projekte unter Druck zu setzen, und wurde von vielen Teilnehmenden ausdrücklich als zentraler Erfolgsfaktor genannt.

WalkMe Learning Arc – Die neue Lernplattform im SAP‑Kontext

WalkMe Learning Arc wurde auf allen Veranstaltungen ausführlich demonstriert und traf auf großes Interesse. Das System stellt die nächste Generation von Lernplattformen im SAP‑Umfeld dar: AI‑nativ, medienflexibel und eng mit der WalkMe DAP‑Erfahrung verwoben.

Inhalte werden nicht mehr als einzelne Seiten (Slides) präsentiert, sondern in moderner Form als blog-artiger „endless scroll“. Microlearnings, also kleine Lernatome und strukturierte Kurse bilden die Grundlage des Datenmodells. „Learning Journeys“, die in künftigen Releases bereitstehen werden, werden darüber hinaus komplexere Lernpfade für unterschiedliche Rollen, Kenntnisstände oder Interessengebiete möglich machen.

Die Erstellung von Lerncontent wird durch KI wesentlich beschleunigt. Das System analysiert Screenshots, Medien und UI‑Elemente, generiert Alternativtexte für Barrierefreiheit, interpretiert Inhalt und Logik aus Bildschirmaufzeichnungen und übersetzt Textfragmente lokal im Browser. Besonders großes Interesse zeigten die Teilnehmer an der inhaltlichen Interpretation des Screenrecordings: Aus den so gewonnenen Informationen können ohne Autorenaufwand beschreibende Texte oder Quizfragen abgeleitet werden. Diese nachhaltige KI‑Unterstützung führt zu einer völlig neuen Art des Autorenarbeit und die Teilnehmer bestätigen den massiven Zeitgewinn, den dieser Ansatz mit sich bringt.

Als besonders beeindruckend wurde darüber hinaus die Möglichkeit wahrgenommen, Lernobjekte prompt-basiert zu erzeugen. Ein Prompt wie „Erstelle ein Microlearning zur Erstellung einer Bestellanforderung in SAP S/4HANA Cloud“ reicht aus, um daraus strukturierte Lernschritte, erläuternde Texte und, sofern gewünscht, ein Storyboard für die eigentliche Systemsimulation zu entwickeln. Durch KI-basierte Interpretation und Zusammenführung verschiedener Quelldokumente – PDFs, Prozessmodelle, Screenshots, Videos oder bestehenden Lerncontent aus anderen Quellen – ergibt sich ein Produktionsprozess mit weit weniger manuellem Aufwand, Autorenteams können sich so stärker auf Kuratierung und inhaltliche Validierung konzentrieren.

Die WalkMe‑Komponente „WalkMe Menu“ übernimmt die kontextsensitive Bereitstellung der Inhalte, die bislang Aufgabe des SAP Companion war. Inhalt kann wie bisher direkt im System angezeigt werden, sobald Nutzer:innen sich in der entsprechenden App oder Transaktion befinden. Diese enge Verzahnung von Lernen und Arbeiten ist und bleibt ein zentraler Aspekt.

Das rollenbasierte WalkMe Learning Arc Lernportal dient als zentraler Delivery-Hub für Lerninhalte. Die KI‑gestützte Suche identifiziert relevante Lernobjekte, auch wenn Nutzer:innen nur vage Formulierungen eingeben.

Auch im Hinblick auf Layout und Design entwickelt sich Learning Arc schnell weiter. Basisfarben, Logos, Sprechblasenstile und andere visuelle Elemente sind bereits an die Unternehmens-CI anpassbar.

Roadmap WalkMe Learning Arc – Ein Blick nach vorn

Der Funktionsumfang wird in den kommenden Releases weiterwachsen. Learning Journeys, Versionierung, Zertifizierungen, ein erweiterter Übersetzungsworkflow, SCORM‑Tracking und der Ausbau der Medienextraktion über KI bilden wichtige Bausteine

AI-assisted User Learning and Change Management – KI im Zentrum moderner Lernstrategien

Das KI-‑gestützte Werkzeug AI-assisted User Learning and Change Management ergänzt das SAP-Learning ‑Portfolio um eine Analyse- und Strukturierungskomponente, die der Content-Produktion vorgelagert ist. Während WalkMe Learning Arc Lerninhalte erstellt, bereitstellt und den Lernfortschritt sowie die User Adoption misst, identifiziert das KI ‑Werkzeug, wo im Rahmen eines Transformationsprojekts Lernbedarf besteht und welche Rollen in die einzelnen Geschäftsprozesse eingebunden sind. Auf dieser Basis entwickelt die Lösung rollenspezifische Lernpläne, bestimmt geeignete Lernformate und erstellt Inhaltsempfehlungen auf Grundlage von SAP-Know-how und kundenspezifischen Projektdaten.

 

Hierfür nutzt das Tool Informationen aus SAP Best Practices sowie kundenspezifischen Input aus Systemen wie SAP Signavio oder SAP Cloud ALM. Zusätzlich können weitere kundenspezifische Dokumente eingebunden werden – beispielsweise Fit-Gap-Analysen, Rollenbeschreibungen oder Prozessdesign-Dokumente.

Auf Basis von Prozesskomplexität, Priorisierung und Kritikalität leitet die KI die jeweiligen Lernbedarfe und vorgeschlagenen Inhalte ab. Der erzeugte Output ist äußerst umfassend: Er reicht von rollenbasierten Curricula und strukturierten Lernpfaden über Lernmodule bis hin zu textbasierten Inhalten einzelner Assets wie Präsentationsfolien, Storyboards für Lernvideos, Quizzen oder Simulationen.

Als besonders hilfreich erweist sich auch die automatische Identifikation von Änderungsbedarfen an Lernobjekten im Projektverlauf. Das System erkennt, welche Lernobjekte von Funktions- oder Prozessänderungen betroffen sind und daher überprüft oder angepasst werden müssen. Die KI schlägt passende Änderungen vor, die anschließend von Prozessexperten validiert und freigegeben werden. Dadurch kann ein gesamtes Lern-Repository kontinuierlich und messbar aktuell gehalten werden.

Das Werkzeug unterstützt Unternehmen effektiv dabei zu bestimmen, welche Trainingsmaßnahmen für ein Transformationsprojekt oder später pro Release erforderlich sind. Dadurch entfällt der übliche Zeitdruck kurz vor GoLive, der häufig projektkritische Ressourcen belastet. Die Content-Produktion kann deutlich früher geplant und gestartet werden. In kommenden Releases sollen zudem klassische OCM Funktionalitäten – darunter Kommunikationsplanung, Stakeholder Management und Change‑ Impact‑ Analysen – integriert werden.

Lizenzierung und Nutzung erfolgen über den SAP MicroApp Hub. Die Abrechnung auf Basis von AI Units ermöglicht eine transparente Kosten- und Budgetplanung.

Fazit

Die diesjährige Veranstaltungsreihe hat eindrucksvoll verdeutlicht, wie dynamisch und vielschichtig sich die Verbindung von Lernen, KI-Technologie und Digital Adoption entwickelt. In einer wie immer durchweg positiven, freundschaftlichen und konstruktiven Atmosphäre tauschten sich Teilnehmer:innen und Vortragende intensiv über die neuesten Innovationen aus. Die Weiterentwicklung von WalkMe Learning Arc und die Einbindung von KI-gestützten Werkzeugen wie AI-assisted User Learning and Change Management wurden von den Kund:innen mit großem Interesse und wertvollem Feedback bewertet. Die Lösungen ermöglichen Unternehmen eine zielgerichtete, personalisierte und effiziente Gestaltung von Lerninhalten und Veränderungsprozessen. Dank Automatisierung und Integration von SAP Best Practices sowie kundenspezifischen Informationen entsteht eine hochrelevante Lernlandschaft, die auch Governance- und Aktualitätsfragen adressiert. Die geplante Erweiterung um klassische OCM-Funktionen stößt auf hohe Erwartungen innerhalb der Community. Insgesamt wurde deutlich, dass innovative Lernlösungen einen wesentlichen Beitrag zur erfolgreichen digitalen Transformation und zum effektiven Change Management leisten können – und die offene, engagierte Diskussion im Rahmen der Roundtables war ein starker Impulsgeber für zukünftige Entwicklungen. Gleichzeitig wurde klargestellt, dass SEN bis 2030 ein verlässlicher Anker bleibt. Der Umstieg auf WalkMe Learning Arc kann bewusst geplant und umgesetzt werden.

Die intensiven Diskussionen der Community zeigten deutlich den Wunsch nach weiteren Austauschformaten, nach Best Practices und nach Orientierung bei Governance Fragen.

FAQ

SEN nach 2030

  • SEN abspielbar, aber nicht editierbar
  • Wartungsende zum 30.11.2030
  • Versionen und ältere Stände müssen vorab exportiert werden

Transition‑Mechanik

  • Workspace‑Transfer via API
  • Struktur wird übernommen, Versionen nicht
  • Segmentierungen müssen neu angelegt werden

Companion / In‑App‑Help

  • Wird nicht migriert
  • WalkMe Embedded ersetzt Companion
  • Guided Tours müssen neu gebaut werden

Kontextsensitive Ausspielung

  • Über WalkMe Menu
  • Bestandteil der WalkMe Learning Arc SKU
  • Funktioniert auch ohne volles WalkMe DAP‑Paket
  • Kontextinformationen bestehender SEN Contents werden übernommen

Learning Arc – Medien & KI

  • Unterstützt Screenshots, Videos, Screenrecordings
  • Automatische Analyse durch KI
  • Prompt‑basiertes Authoring
  • Barrierefreiheit über Alt‑Texte

Übersetzung

  • Echtzeitübersetzung über Chromium Translator API (lokal, DSGVO‑konform)
  • Screenshots werden nicht „übersetzt“
  • XLIFF und manuelle Übersetzung folgen

Hosting der KI

  • Private KI-Modelle ohne Training an Kundendaten

AI‑assisted User Learning and Change Management

  • Nutzt Signavio-Prozessmodelle, SAP Best Practices, CALM und unstrukturierte Dokumente
  • Identifiziert Rollen, Lernbedarf, basierend auf Change‑Impact und Prozesskritikalität
  • Generiert rollenspezifische Lernpläne, und Rohinhalt als Grundlage für Videos, E‑Learnings und Klassenraumschulungen

entry.title: Join the SAP Business AI in Utilities - Partner Live Expert Session
entry.content:
Dear Partners, 

The Utilities industry is undergoing rapid transformation. Rising customer expectations, the transition to renewable energy, increased regulatory pressure, and a growing need for operational efficiency are driving utilities to rethink how they run their business. Artificial Intelligence has moved from a promising concept to a practical accelerator—and SAP Business AI is at the heart of this evolution.

To support partners in navigating this changing landscape, we are excited to announce our upcoming SAP Business AI in Utilities – Partner Live Expert Session, taking place on:

📅13 April 2026

🕘09:00 and 16:00 (Europe/Paris)

Duration: 90 minutes 

This session will cover how SAP Business AI in Utilities can help your customers become smarter, faster and more productive. 

The agenda is as follows:

  • Quick overview of the Utilities sector with key themes and market analysis
  • An in-depth presentation on Business AI in Utilities including use cases and references
  • How to sell AI into Utilities (how the AI units work, who to talk to for more help etc.)
  • A quick demonstration of AI Utilities demos on SDE 2.0
  • Q & A and next steps (more tailored training)

Learning Objectives:
Understand the Utilities sector and how SAP Business AI can help you as a partner

Roles:
Customer Engagement Executive, Decision Maker / Management, Presales, Project Manager, Sales

 
No prerequisites required—all partners interested in AI in Utilities are welcome.
 

AI is reshaping every part of the Utilities value chain—from grid operations to customer service—and partners play a crucial role in driving this transformation forward.
Join us for this expert session and strengthen your ability to guide customers through their AI journey with SAP.

👉Secure your spot today: Register Now

 
If you have issues to register, please click here

A change file was created but the button just reappears after reloading. After some digging I found that probably is done by manifest change. 

{
	"changeType": "appdescr_ui_generic_app_changePageConfiguration",
	"content": {
		"parentPage": {
			"component": "sap.suite.ui.generic.template.ListReport",
			"entitySet": "C_ObjPgTechnicalObject"
